#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPArchitecture #SAPBusinessDataCloud #SAPPartners #DataDriven

entry.title: From XSUAA to AMS: The Practical Path to Stronger Authorization
entry.content:
(Jana Subramanian serves as APJ Principal Cybersecurity Advisor and Compliance and has been recognized as a Fellow of Information Privacy (FIP) by the International Association of Privacy Professionals (IAPP). As part of his responsibilities, Jana helps with strategic customer engagements related to topics such as cybersecurity, data privacy, secure data flow, multi-cloud security integration architecture, contractual assurance, audit, and compliance.)

Introduction

SAP BTP security architecture is often explained in fragments: authentication here, authorization there, principal propagation somewhere else. But in real enterprise landscapes, these are tightly connected flows—and the exact sequencing matters. A small misunderstanding in where tokens are issued, how identities are propagated, or where decisions are enforced can quickly turn into inconsistent access, brittle integrations, and gaps in traceability, especially in hybrid (cloud + on-premises) environments.

In this blog, I’ll provide a comprehensive technical walkthrough of two complementary authorization models on SAP BTP, using numbered, end-to-end flows directly mapped to architecture diagrams:

  1. XSUAA-Based Flow — authentication, token routing, and principal propagation to on-premises systems
  2. AMS-Based Flow — centralized, policy-based authorization with CAP CDS enforcement

From there, we’ll zoom out to the “why”: where XSUAA fits best, why XSUAA alone can become limiting at enterprise scale, and what Authorization Management Service (AMS) adds—especially when you need consistent policy enforcement across applications, clearer separation of duties, and stronger auditability.

For the sake of clarity, this post intentionally stays at a high architectural level: I focus on concepts, sequencing, and decision points, and do not go into step-by-step configuration. The goal is to build a clear mental model you can reuse—before diving into implementation details in your own landscape.

XSUAA-Based Flow: End-to-End Identity and Principal Propagation to SAP S/4HANA (On-Prem)

This architectural pattern shows how a real end-user identity securely accesses an SAP BTP application and is then propagated to SAP S/4HANA on-premises—without falling back to shared technical users. That end-to-end identity continuity is a key foundation for zero-trust design principles and for auditable user accountability, especially in hybrid (cloud + on-prem) landscapes.

The goal is simple: the user who clicks in the browser is the same user recorded in SAP S/4HANA logs and authorization checks.

Note for clarity: This section focuses on the architecture and sequencing at a high level. Detailed configuration steps are intentionally out of scope to keep the mental model clear and reusable.

Systems involved

This XSUAA-based flow spans multiple components across cloud and on-premise boundaries:

  • Corporate Identity Provider (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID, Okta) – enterprise authentication source (password, MFA, conditional access)
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) – federation proxy between corporate IdP and SAP BTP trust
  • XSUAA (Authorization and Trust Management) – OAuth2/OIDC token service for SAP BTP apps (JWT issuance, scopes, role collections)
  • AppRouter + Application Runtime – application entry point, session handling, and token forwarding
  • Destination & Connectivity – secure routing and token exchange for outbound calls
  • SAP Cloud Connector – controlled, outbound tunnel into the on-premise network
  • SAP S/4HANA (ABAP) – backend authorization enforcement using ABAP roles and authorization objects

Figure 1: Authentication, token routing, and principal propagation via XSUAA

 

Architecture diagram: XSUAA flow

 Authentication steps (blue phase)

Step 1 — User access

The user opens the SAP BTP application in a browser. AppRouter acts as the entry point and checks whether a valid session already exists.

Step 2 — AppRouter triggers authentication via XSUAA

If no session is present, AppRouter redirects the user to XSUAA (/oauth/authorize). XSUAA then redirects to IAS as the trusted identity provider—following the OAuth2 authorization code flow:

  • AppRouter → XSUAA (/oauth/authorize)
  • XSUAA → IAS (trusted IdP)

Step 3 — IAS federates to the corporate IdP

IAS forwards authentication to the corporate IdP. This ensures:

  • password/MFA and corporate policies stay with the enterprise IdP
  • no user credentials enter SAP BTP

Step 4 — Authentication assertion returned

After validation, the corporate IdP returns a signed assertion/token to IAS containing the authenticated identity and relevant claims.

Step 5 — IAS issues an SAP-trusted identity token

IAS validates the corporate IdP response and returns a SAML assertion (or OIDC response, depending on setup) that XSUAA trusts for user authentication. XSUAA then issues the JWT access token.

Step 6 — Authorization code exchange and JWT issuance

XSUAA exchanges the authorization code for a JWT access token:

  • includes user identity, scopes, and role collections
  • is forwarded to AppRouter and the application runtime
    At this point, authentication is complete and authorization context is represented in the token.

Token and routing steps (green phase)

Step 7 — Destination selected

When the app needs to call SAP S/4HANA, it resolves the target destination (host, authentication type, proxy settings) via the Destination service.

Step 8 — Destination token exchange for backend compatibility

Based on destination configuration, SAP BTP performs the required token exchange to produce a backend-compatible credential, such as:

  • OAuth2 SAML bearer / assertion-based flows
  • principal propagation material (cert-based)
  • short-lived, target-specific credentials (not reused across systems)
    Critically, the user context is preserved, enabling traceability end to end.

Step 9 — Connectivity routing boundary

The outbound request is routed through SAP Connectivity service, enforcing the key boundary:
on-premise systems are not opened for inbound access from the cloud.

Principal propagation steps (red phase)

Step 10 — Secure tunnel via SAP Cloud Connector

The request traverses into the on-premise network via SAP Cloud Connector, using mutually authenticated transport and explicitly configured trust. This ensures:

  • cloud identity is cryptographically verified
  • only permitted resources are reachable
  • connectivity remains controlled and outbound-initiated

Step 11 — Principal mapped in SAP S/4HANA

Inside SAP S/4HANA, the propagated identity is mapped to an ABAP user (for example via certificate mapping rules). The important outcome is:

  • no shared technical user is required
  • the user identity remains intact across the boundary

Step 12 — Authorization enforced in ABAP

SAP S/4HANA performs authorization using native ABAP mechanisms:

  • ABAP roles/profiles
  • authorization objects (e.g., S_TCODE, business objects, org-level checks)
  • application-level authorization checks

Outcome: SAP S/4HANA logs and authorization traces reflect the real end user, not a generic system account—supporting strong accountability and audit readiness.

Key security benefits

  • True SSO experience across cloud and on-premise
  • End-to-end audit trail mapped to real users
  • No shared credentials with broad access
  • Authorization enforced where data lives (ABAP stays authoritative for ABAP)
  • Clear trust boundary with controlled, outbound-only on-prem connectivity

Part 2: AMS-Based Authentication and Centralized Authorization

In Part 1, we walked through an end-to-end XSUAA-based pattern where a user signs in through the enterprise identity provider and the application receives a trusted access token for calling services and backends. Part 2 keeps the end-user SSO experience (Corporate IdP → IAS) consistent, while shifting authorization to centrally managed policies. Authorization decisions are driven by centrally managed policies, not scattered across application-specific logic.

With Authorization Management Service (AMS), policy definition and evaluation are externalized into a central service. Your CAP application then enforces those decisions consistently through CDS-based authorization (for example, using CAP annotations such as @requires and @restrict). This approach helps teams implement fine-grained and consistent authorization across services while improving governance and auditability because policies can be reviewed and managed in one place.

Note for clarity: This section stays intentionally high-level and architecture-focused. Detailed configuration steps are not included, so the flow is easier to understand and apply across different landscapes.

Systems involved

  1. This AMS-based flow spans the following components:
  2. Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Enterprise authentication source (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID, Okta)
  3. SAP Cloud Identity Services
  4. Identity Authentication (IAS) – federation and login orchestration
  5. Identity Directory – user/group/attribute context used for policy evaluation (often synchronized from upstream identity sources)
  6. Authorization Management Service (AMS) Central policy store and policy evaluation engine
  7. CAP Application (with AMS integration) Application runtime that enforces policy outcomes via CAP CDS-based authorization (e.g., entities/actions annotated with @requires / @restrict)
  8. Destination Service Target-specific token exchange and connectivity preparation for backend calls (when required)

Architecture diagram: AMS flow

Figure 2: AMS authentication and centralized policy-based authorization flow

Authentication steps (blue phase)

The authentication phase remains the same as the XSUAA-based pattern—preserving the user experience and existing corporate IdP integrations.

Step 1 — End-user access The user opens the SAP BTP application. The application entry point (AppRouter + CAP runtime) checks for an active session.

Step 2 — AppRouter redirects to IAS If no session is present, AppRouter initiates sign-in using OIDC and redirects the user to Identity Authentication (IAS).

Step 3 — IAS federates to the corporate IdP IAS forwards authentication to the corporate identity provider using SAML or OIDC federation. Enterprise authentication controls (such as MFA and conditional access policies) are enforced at the corporate IdP.

Step 4 — User authenticates The user successfully authenticates with enterprise credentials. The corporate IdP validates the authentication request.

Step 5 — Assertion/response returned to IAS The corporate IdP returns a signed SAML assertion or OIDC response to IAS confirming the authenticated identity.

Step 6 — The application receives a trusted access token After successful sign-in, the application receives a trusted access token (commonly a JWT) representing the user identity and session context. The token is forwarded to the CAP application runtime.

Centralized authorization using AMS (green phase)

This is where AMS differs from a “token-only” approach. Tokens establish who the user is; AMS policies determine what the user is allowed to do and see. Instead of embedding authorization checks throughout each application, CAP can delegate policy evaluation to AMS and enforce the outcome consistently at runtime.

Step 7 —The CAP AMS integration (plugin) resolves the user’s assigned AMS policies and injects roles/filters for the request; CAP then enforces authorization via CDS rules.

Step 8 — Identity context and attributes resolved AMS builds the authorization context needed for evaluation, typically based on:

  • user attributes (e.g., department, region, cost center—depending on what your landscape provides)
  • group memberships (from Identity Directory and/or upstream identity sources)
  • policy assignments (which policies apply to the user and the requested resource)

Step 9 — Policies evaluated AMS evaluates centrally managed policies and determines the authorization outcome, such as:

  • permit/deny decisions for actions and services
  • fine-grained constraints and conditions (for example, patterns that can support entity/field/instance-level controls where applicable)

Step 10 — Decision returned and enforced in CAP The authorization outcome is returned to the CAP runtime and enforced consistently by the application:

  • decisions may be cached where appropriate for performance
  • only authorized requests proceed to data access and downstream calls This preserves a clean separation: policy is centrally managed and evaluated; enforcement happens reliably at runtime.

Backend access (purple phase)

Authorization is decided and enforced at the application/API layer first. If the authorized request requires backend access, the application then obtains target-specific credentials.

Step 11 — Target-specific token exchange (Destination Service) If the CAP application must call a backend (e.g., SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Business Data Cloud, SuccessFactors, or internal BTP services), Destination Service performs the appropriate, target-specific token exchange based on the configured trust relationship—issuing short-lived, target-compatible credentials.

Backend systems continue to enforce their own native authorization controls. AMS primarily standardizes and centralizes authorization at the application/API layer to reduce drift across applications and improve governance.

Trust relationships in the AMS architecture

A typical trust chain includes:

  1. BTP subaccount with IAS tenant (trust configuration for authentication)
  2. IAS with  corporate IdP (federation via SAML/OIDC)
  3. Destination Service with target system (target-specific trust and token exchange)
  4. AMS with Identity Directory (groups/attributes and policy assignments used for evaluation)

Why AMS matters at enterprise scale

As landscapes grow, authorization logic often becomes inconsistent across apps: duplicated rules, different interpretations of “who can do what,” and uneven auditability. AMS addresses this by enabling:

  • Centralized policy management – less drift across multiple services and teams
  • Consistent enforcement – CAP applies policy outcomes uniformly via CDS-based authorization
  • Fine-grained control – more expressive policies beyond coarse-grained token scopes
  • Stronger governance and auditability – policies can be reviewed, versioned, and managed centrally

Part 3: When AMS Beats XSUAA-Only Authorization at Enterprise Scale

Choosing between “XSUAA-only” authorization and an AMS-driven approach is an important architectural decision in enterprise SAP BTP landscapes. The key is understanding what each service is optimized for:

  1. XSUAA primarily provides identity and token-based authorization context (roles/scopes represented in the token).
  2. AMS primarily provides policy-based authorization decisions: what a user is allowed to do, under which conditions, and why.

In practice, XSUAA is excellent for authentication and issuing trusted access tokens, while AMS is designed to scale authorization governance through centralized policy definition and evaluation—especially when requirements become dynamic, conditional, and organization-wide.

 

Note on technical accuracy: In CAP, you’ll still use authorization constructs such as @requires and @restrict. The difference is that CAP remains the enforcement point, while AMS centralizes the policy decision logic and governance, reducing duplication and drift across applications.

Problems AMS solves

1) Role explosion

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

XSUAA authorization is commonly modeled with role collections and scopes. At enterprise scale, access needs quickly diversify by region, business unit, data domain, sensitivity level, and job function. If you try to represent all combinations as static roles (for example, Sales_Manager_EMEA_Read vs. Sales_Manager_APAC_Write), the number of roles can grow rapidly—making access management harder to maintain, review, and keep consistent.

How AMS helps:

AMS supports policy-based authorization where access rules can be expressed in terms of attributes, groups, and conditions. Instead of multiplying static roles, you can capture intent once, for example:

“Sales managers can access sales data for their assigned region.” Access is then evaluated dynamically based on user attributes/group assignments and policy conditions—reducing role sprawl and improving governance.

2) Hard-coded or application-specific authorization

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

Even when CAP annotations are used, real-world authorization often becomes application-specific: additional checks in handlers, custom logic per service, and authorization rules scattered across multiple codebases. When access requirements change, updates frequently require code changes, retesting, and redeployment, which slows governance and increases the risk of inconsistent implementations.

How AMS helps:

AMS enables teams to externalize authorization decision logic into centrally managed policies. CAP can enforce those outcomes consistently, while policy changes can be introduced centrally—reducing the need for every application team to re-implement or duplicate the same authorization logic. This reduces coupling between policy evolution and application release cycles.

3) Inconsistent enforcement across services and channels

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

When each application implements authorization slightly differently, the same user can receive different access outcomes depending on which application or API is used. Over time, this creates gaps, exceptions, and “shadow rules” that are difficult to detect—and even harder to justify.

How AMS helps:

With AMS, authorization logic becomes centrally defined and can be applied consistently across CAP services and APIs that enforce the same centrally governed policy model. This improves uniformity of access decisions and reduces the risk of policy drift between applications.

4) Audit opacity: “Why does this user have access?”

The challenge (XSUAA-only):

When authorization is split across role assignments, token scopes, and application-specific checks, it becomes difficult to answer audit questions such as:

“Why does this user have access to this data?” The answer often requires correlating role collections, reviewing code paths, and interpreting differences across multiple services.

How AMS helps:

AMS makes authorization more reviewable and explainable because policies are expressed as centrally managed declarations. This improves audit readiness: reviewers can assess policy intent directly, track changes over time, and relate authorization outcomes back to policy logic—without needing to reverse-engineer authorization behavior from multiple application deployments.

Aspect

XSUAA

AMS

Primary purpose

Identity and trusted token issuance; token-based authorization context (roles/scopes)

Central policy-based authorization decisions and governance

Authorization model

Role collections / scopes (token-centric)

Policy-based (commonly enforced through CAP CDS authorization patterns)

Where rules live

Often split between role assignments + application-specific checks

Centralized policies, enforced consistently at runtime

Granularity

Typically coarse-grained (role/scope level)

More fine-grained and conditional access patterns (including instance/attribute-driven controls where applicable)

Change management

Changes often require adjusting roles and/or application logic

Many access changes (policy assignment/composition) can be handled centrally; changes to the underlying CDS authorization model still require normal application change control.

Consistency across apps

Can drift if each app implements checks differently

Reduces drift through centralized policy intent and evaluation

Audit readiness

Harder to explain “why” across roles + code

Easier to review policy intent and changes centrally

Part 4: How AMS Meets Compliance Requirements

SAP Authorization Management Service (AMS) is built for enterprise-scale authorization governance: central policy definition, consistent enforcement, and reviewable access intent. That combination maps well to common assurance and internal control expectations—especially where organizations must demonstrate who can access what, under which conditions, and how those rules are managed over time.

Separation of Duties (SoD)

Assurance context: In many organizations, SOC 1 and SOC 2 expectations (and internal control requirements) include demonstrating separation between individuals who can initiate, approve, and execute sensitive business transactions—particularly when those processes impact financial reporting or operational assurance.

How AMS helps: With centralized, policy-based authorization, SoD constraints can be expressed as explicit, reviewable rules (for example, “an approver must not approve their own request”) and enforced consistently at runtime—without relying on ad-hoc checks scattered across application code.

Principle of Least Privilege

Regulatory context: Privacy and security frameworks commonly require access to be limited to the minimum necessary. For example, GDPR Article 25 (“data protection by design and by default”) emphasizes default protections and limiting unnecessary accessibility.

How AMS helps: AMS supports least-privilege patterns through policy conditions such as:

  • Conditional access — allow only when business conditions are met (e.g., status, ownership, org scope)
  • Context-aware decisions — incorporate user attributes (e.g., department, region, cost center) to avoid broad entitlements
  • Time-bound access — model temporary access by policy condition (e.g., time windows) to reduce standing privilege

Result: access can be scoped precisely to job function and context, rather than granted broadly via static roles.

Auditability and transparency

Assurance context: In assurance engagements such as SOC 1 / SOC 2, organizations typically need controls to be documented, reviewable, and operationally verifiable—including the ability to justify why particular access is granted.

How AMS helps: Centralized policy-based authorization improves audit readiness because:

  • Policies are centralized — authorization intent is easier to review than logic distributed across multiple codebases
  • Policy lifecycle is governable — change management practices (review/approval, versioning, traceability) can be applied to policy updates
  • Reduced dependency on deployments — policy review can be performed independently of application release cycles, minimizing the need to “read code” to understand authorization intent

In short: it becomes much easier to answer, “Why does this user have access?” by pointing to centrally governed policy intent rather than piecing together multiple implementations.

Zero Trust alignment

Framework context: Zero Trust shifts away from implicit trust and toward continuous verification and explicit authorization decisions for each access request.

How AMS helps: AMS aligns well with core Zero Trust principles:

  • Authenticated — identity is established through corporate IdP federation (commonly via IAS in SAP BTP architectures)
  • Explicitly authorized — access is evaluated against centrally governed policy intent (no “implicit trust”)
  • Evaluated at runtime — authorization is applied at the time of access, not only at session start

This supports a practical Zero Trust posture where every request is evaluated against policy and context.

 

Framework / standard

Typical requirement

How AMS helps (capability)

SOC 1

Controls relevant to services affecting financial reporting (e.g., controlled access, segregation expectations)

Central policy intent, enforceable conditions, consistent authorization outcomes

SOC 2

Security and access control expectations (governed controls, reviewability)

Centralized policy governance and consistent enforcement

GDPR

Data protection by design/default; minimize unnecessary access

Least-privilege policies and contextual access scoping

NIS2

Risk management measures and access control expectations

Central governance of access rules and consistent enforcement

DORA

ICT risk management expectations; protection and prevention controls

Governable policy lifecycle and reviewable authorization intent

ISO/IEC 27001

Access control expectations; least privilege; reviewability

Attribute-/condition-based authorization patterns and centralized policy management

Conclusion

XSUAA remains a strong foundation on SAP BTP for establishing application trust and issuing tokens that carry identity and role/scope context in XSUAA-based architectures.

As landscapes grow—more apps, more teams, more data domains, and more audit scrutiny—authorization becomes the harder problem. Enterprise requirements increasingly demand consistent decisions, fine-grained controls, and a clear way to explain why access was granted.

This is where Authorization Management Service (AMS) strengthens the architecture by enabling:

  • Centralized, policy-based authorization that scales without role explosion
  • Improved governance support through consistent policy management and clearer traceability for audits (e.g., SOC/ISO) and regulatory expectations (GDPR, NIS2, DORA)
  • Clear separation of concerns: identity (who you are), authorization policy (what you can do under which conditions), and application enforcement
  • Consistent runtime enforcement in the application layer (e.g., CAP CDS @requires / @restrict) based on assigned policies and context

Future-proof security architecture for SAP BTP

  • Target state (new CAP apps): IAS for authentication + AMS for centralized authorization policy
  • Coexistence: XSUAA remains relevant for existing apps/scenarios; adoption can be incremental

In short: keep sign-in consistent, modernize authorization where it matters most, and scale governance without scaling complexity.

References:

SAP BTP Developer Guide: XSUAA to AMS migration

AMS vs XSUAA: The Future of Authentication and Authorization in SAP BTP

CAP Authorization Documentation

entry.title: Unpacking SAP Business Data Cloud for Solution Architects
entry.content:
Data everywhere.  AI expectations skyrocketing.  Architects caught in the middle.  Sound familiar?

In Unpacking SAP Business Data Cloud for solution architects, we cut through the noise and show how SAP BDC brings order to modern data chaos—fast.  One unified architecture.  Trusted data products.  Built-in governance.  And yes, AI and analytics that actually run on real business data.

In this session, you’ll get a sharp overview of SAP Business Data Cloud, why data products matter, and how capabilities like zero-copy sharing, object store persistence, and SAP Databricks integration fit together.  We’ll top it off with a live demo of an intelligent application and a walkthrough of the data product catalog, metadata, and lifecycle management.

Plus—and this is key—we’ll zoom in on partner best practices and where you can create differentiated value for customers.

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Control Tower: Product Updates (HY2-2025) and Road Map Highlights (HY1-2026)
entry.content:
Introduction

Sustainability reporting is shifting from annual disclosure to continuous, operational performance management, and that requires sustainability intelligence that runs inside the business, not beside it. SAP’s 2026 position is straightforward: sustainability AI must run where the business runs, inside your ERP, or it won’t be truly intelligent, scalable, or manageable.

SAP Sustainability Control Tower supports this by grounding sustainability insights in a trusted enterprise context and embedding them into the end‑to‑end lifecycle of scope definition, governed data collection, analysis, and AI‑assisted reporting, to enable faster, lower‑effort compliance and measurable business impact.

With that framing, the updates in HY2 2025 focus on strengthening the end-to-end path from scope → data → insight → disclosure, and our HY1 2026 roadmap continues to expand regulatory coverage, auditability, data quality controls, and AI-enabled reporting so that customers can achieve faster, lower-cost compliance with measurable business impact.

Q3 & Q4 2025 Highlights for SAP Sustainability Control Tower

Content Factory: Accelerating regulatory metric content at scale

To support customers on their ESRS journey, SAP SCT continues to expand SAP‑provided sustainability metric content, covering both quantitative and qualitative ESRS requirements, while reducing duplication and improving mapping quality.

 

We significantly increased ESRS coverage to help customers meet ESRS data-point requirements, with clear visibility into coverage progress. As communicated in our Help Portal, SAP SCT’s ESRS datapoint coverage and the number of released/planned metrics increased substantially through 2025, with the aim to enable ESRS reporting based on a strong data foundation and sourcing capabilities.

Reporting Scope Management: Turning DMA results into actionable data requirements

ESG data collection relies on a clear reporting scope, usually based on a company’s double materiality assessment (DMA), to determine what to report, when, how, and by whom. SAP SCT’s Reporting Scope features help turn disclosure requirements into specific, system-managed data needs.

With Reporting Scope Management, you can:

  • Capture the outcome of materiality assessments (“what to report”) and map it to SAP-provided ESRS metrics or your own custom metrics.
  • Define scope items across organizational structures and reporting cycles so the scope becomes the backbone for workflows, analysis, and reporting.
  • Ensure traceable collection and reporting over periods to make scope changes transparent and auditable over time.

 

Formula-based metric definition: Configure sustainability KPIs with a flexible Formula Builder

Sustainability reporting rarely ends with out-of-the-box metrics alone. That’s why SCT supports the creation of custom sustainability metrics with the same metadata richness as SAP‑provided metrics, and now with a Formula Builder that makes defining calculation logic far more accessible.

With a formula-based metric definition, you can:

  • Create custom sustainability metrics that are not part of SAP delivery and assign consistent metadata.
  • Define variables mapped to source measures and apply dimension filters (for example, restrict to a specific entity, location, or category).
  • Use the formula builder to define expressions that support arithmetic, conditional, and aggregation logic, helping you align internal KPIs or industry-specific requirements with your reporting model.

Analyze ESG Data app: Personalized insights, drilldowns, and governed access

In HY2 2025, we continued to evolve the analytics experience with the Analyze ESG Data application, a flexible, personalized way to explore both SAP-provided and custom metrics, replacing static KPI tiles with a more dynamic analysis layer.

 Key capabilities include:

  • Drilldown analysis across organizational and metric dimensions (for example, business location, reporting line, legal entity, time period, and predefined metric dimensions).
  • Filtering and grouping by time, hierarchy, sustainability matter, reporting frameworks (like ESRS / EU Taxonomy), or user-defined categories.
  • Personalization: save and share views to align analysis across stakeholders and teams.
  • Governance: instance-based authorization supports secure data access and separation of duties.

Enriched AI-based ESG reporting: Customizable templates + in-app AI actions

SAP SCT’s AI-assisted reporting capabilities continue to mature, focusing on reducing manual effort while increasing control, structure, and reusability. In HY2 2025, we strengthened report generation with:

  • User-defined templates to structure ESG reports and include SAP‑shipped quantitative metrics alongside charts and tables from a pre-shipped list.
  • AI actions for in-app editing, enabling users to refine generated text (e.g., shorten or lengthen it) directly within the report.

This allows sustainability teams to move quickly from data to narrative while maintaining consistent, reviewable reporting results that align with internal disclosure requirements.

Our Initiatives: Linking sustainability ambition to execution

SAP SCT is designed to help organizations move beyond compliance into performance management, including tracking sustainability initiatives with transparency into benefits and costs. Our Initiatives App capabilities focus on turning goals into actionable work with traceability.

With Our Initiatives, organizations can:

  • Create and manage initiatives across lifecycle statuses with detailed metadata (impacted metrics, financials, benefits, actions).
  • Capture CapEx / OpEx, business-as-usual comparisons, and sustainability impact with system-supported calculations and guidance.
  • Break initiatives into actions, assign owners, and track changes to support structured planning and measurable execution.

One API: Unified Sustainability Metrics API for reporting & disclosure consumption

To simplify integration with disclosure tools, reporting platforms, and external consumers, SCT introduced a unified Sustainability Metrics API (One Read API) for metric value consumption.

Key capabilities:

  • Unified retrieval of metric values across quantitative and qualitative metric types.
  • Exposure of metric metadata, including ESG standard references for tagging purposes (important for downstream disclosure).
  • Bulk retrieval based on reporting scope, period, and hierarchy; plus flexibility for time aggregation and hierarchy selection.
  • Ongoing support for current APIs remains, but updates are planned primarily in the unified API going forward.

CPE‑UM value scenarios: extensibility for advanced ESG scenarios

SCT’s value scenarios can be extended with CPE‑UM, which enables read/write access to SCT’s data foundation for advanced customer scenarios.

This helps implement scenarios such as:

  • Sample reporting (read scenario) using a connector architecture for performance and flexibility.
  • Custom metric calculation (read/write) by consuming SCT data and writing back calculated values.
  • Custom data consolidation (write scenario) to operationalize ESG data provisioning into SCT.

 H1 2026 Roadmap Highlights

Forward-looking statement: The following roadmap items are planned and may change.

For HY1 2026, SCT priorities focus on: expanding regulatory coverage, market-ready reporting scope & data collection, enhancing analytics & reporting, and wider adoption & integration options. 

IFRS S1 / S2 coverage (Regulatory expansion & interoperability)

We plan to expand regulatory content beyond ESRS by adopting IFRS S1 and S2 where interoperability exists and by providing preconfigured metric definition content to help customers address investor-oriented baseline disclosure requirements.

Link to Road Map Item

Evidence Management (MVP) for Audit Readiness

To strengthen auditability across the ESG lifecycle, SCT plans an Evidence Management capability (MVP) as a central application to attach, manage, and access documents related to ESG data entries.

Planned core capabilities include:

  • Central repository for attachments linked to reference objects with metadata.
  • Upload/download / metadata updates/deletion, including multiple attachments per object.
  • End-to-end scenario staged across quantitative data and qualitative ESG data, with later integration into data collection forms.

Link to Road Map Item

Data products: Sustainability Metrics (consumption & integration scenarios)

We plan to strengthen “data product”- style consumption and integration scenarios so customers can adopt prebuilt integration paths for key measures (e.g., energy consumption and workforce data), with mapping and low-touch operations.

Link to Road Map Item

DMA process coverage & Reporting Scope enhancements

To operationalize the DMA-to-disclosure pipeline, roadmap items include improvements around reporting scope import/export and interfaces to push DMA lists into reporting scope, plus enhancements in data collection configuration driven by scope.

Link to Road Map Item

Multiple language and format support in AI reporting

Planned enhancements for AI-assisted reporting include:

  • Ability to generate reports in multiple languages.
  • Improved output formats (for example enhanced export options) and more flexible generation options (such as filtering at hierarchy level).

Link to Road Map Item

Custom validation rules & plausibility checks for data import

To improve data quality and reduce the risk of incorrect disclosure, SCT plans configuration options for custom validations and plausibility checks, including rules for value ranges, dimension governance, dependency rules across value combinations, and time-based plausibility checks using historical comparisons and tolerance thresholds.

Link to Road Map Item

Stay tuned for more and check the SCT Roadmap Explorer.

Additional Information

 For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Control Tower, we recommend browsing the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release.

entry.title: Planning your transition paths to SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Planning a move to SAP Business Data Cloud isn’t just a technical exercise.  It’s a business conversation.  And honestly, the right architecture choices early on make everything smoother later.

entry.title: When Innovation Meets Real Business Impact - VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026
entry.content:
A community powered by creativity

The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon has once again shown what happens when passionate people, real business challenges, and SAP Business Technology Platform come together. Teams of customers, partners, and experts turned ideas into tangible solutions that demonstrate how SAP BTP can drive measurable value in day-to-day operations.​

Over the course of the Hackathon, every team brought its own perspective, business context and technical skills – from integration and automation to SAP Business AI and extensions on SAP BTP.

The result: a set of working prototypes that not only impress technically, but are rooted in real customer needs and adoption potential.

Celebrating the 2026 finalists

After intense jury deliberation, three teams earned a spot in the grand final at SYNC 2026 for their strong presentations, clear business impact, and high potential for real-world rollout.​

  • Promocean (supported by SOA People) – “Operational Excellence through SAP Business AI”: A showcase of how SAP BTP and Business AI can streamline operations and enhance decision-making in a very pragmatic way.​

  • Ecotone (supported by Expertum) – “Interface Maintenance”: A smart solution addressing the often underestimated pain of interface management, using SAP BTP to bring transparency, stability and control.​

  • NTT Data – “ProTrack”: A forward-looking use case that leverages SAP BTP to better track, steer, or optimize processes​

These finalists did not just deliver “cool demos”, they delivered stories where business and technology reinforce each other, with SAP BTP at the core as the innovation platform.

A strong field beyond the finalists

Of course, a hackathon is about much more than just three winners. Aarini Consulting, Nederlandse Spoorwegen, and Simac IT & BSC also delivered impressive solutions that made the jury’s job anything but easy.​

Each of these teams tackled real business scenarios and used SAP BTP services to create innovation that can move quickly from prototype to production. The organizers explicitly encourage all teams to work with their coaches on post-hackathon follow-up to drive implementation and adoption, because the real success of a hackathon is measured when the ideas go live.

SYNC 2026: where the stage is yours

As recognition of this milestone, the three finalist teams are invited to present at SYNC 2026, the VNSG Annual Conference on March 12. During the opening session, each finalist will pitch their solution live on stage to the SYNC audience. The audience will then vote to determine the VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon Champion – a great opportunity to showcase innovation, customer impact and the power of SAP BTP in front of the wider SAP community.

Keep the momentum going

For everyone involved – finalists, other teams, coaches and the broader community – this hackathon is a starting point, not an ending. The next steps are about refining solutions, planning production rollouts, and scaling adoption so that the innovation created during the hackathon translates into lasting business impact.​

If you are part of the VNSG or wider SAP community and feel inspired by these stories, take this as a call to action: bring your own business challenges, experiment on SAP BTP and co-innovate with partners and customers.

The SYNC 2026 VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon has proven once more that with the right mix of creativity, collaboration and platform capabilities, you can turn ideas into value – fast...

SAP Business Technology Platform SAP Build SAP Integration Suite

entry.title: Streamline User Targeting with Segments in SAP Feature Flags
entry.content:
Managing feature releases becomes significantly easier when you can organize users into meaningful groups. That's where segments come in, they are reusable lists that let you target specific user groups consistently across multiple feature flags in the same service instance.

What Makes Segments Useful?

Think of segments as smart user lists you create once and reuse everywhere within your Feature Flags service instance. Instead of manually defining the same user group for each feature flag, you can reference a single segment across your entire service instance.

Each segment can include users in two ways:

  • Direct deliveries: Add up to 1,000 specific user IDs.
  • Rule-based targeting: Create up to 15 conditional rules based on user attributes like region or account type.

For more information, see Concepts - Segments.

Creating Your First Segment

Getting started is straightforward:

  1. Open the Feature Flags Dashboard and select your service instance.
  2. Navigate to Segments and choose Create Segment.Create Segment form
  3. Choose a unique name (starting with a letter, using only: lowercase letters (a-z), uppercase letters (A-Z), underscores (_), and digits (0-9)).
  4. Add users either by listing specific IDs or by defining attribute-based rules.
  5. Create your segment.

Your segment is now ready to use across any feature flag in that service instance.

A Few Things to Remember

You can create up to 20 segments per service instance. Keep in mind that segments currently in use can't be deleted, the system will show you where they're referenced if you try. This safeguard prevents accidental disruptions to your active feature flags.

Segments give you the flexibility to manage user targeting at scale while keeping your feature flag configurations clean and maintainable.

entry.title: A.I Assisted Transition at your own Pace: BW7.5 to BDC Pilot Experiences
entry.content:
This blog is part of a series related to a complex BW7.5 customer case where we explored together the opportunity to transition to BDC. You can find the intro blog below, plus the links to the subsequent blogs will be listed here -

  •  Link 1
  • Link 2 
  • Link 3

The Time is Now

At the time of typing this it is still early 2026. The year’s priorities and goals are being set. For many of our BW customers, the end of maintenance deadline set at the end of 2027 for NW 7.5 is looming large. Questions such as the below are being asked –

  • How can we maintain what we have built over many years without rebuilding from the ground up?
  • How can we make use of the latest and greatest from A.I?
  • Can we continue to use our existing skills?
  • Can we remain compliant to our regulatory requirements?

The goal of this series of blogs is to outline what customers on BW 7.5 can do to modernise their mission critical system of engagement at their own pace whilst embracing A.I, ensuring they avoid expensive regret cost migrations to third party platforms that can often spiral, seldom delivering on the initial promise. These blogs are based on first hand experiences in a pilot project for BW7.5 to BDC.

Interested to learn more? Read on

The Problem Statement

BW 7.5 is the last version of the ABAP stack SAP Business Warehouse that was initially released in the late 1990’s, it delivers out of the box capabilities (business content, extractor logic, authorisation concepts etc) to manage complex third normal form data structures from SAP ECC (legacy SAP application around finance, HR, supply chain etc) across a layered scalable architecture (LSA). In 2016, the ‘new’ BW was released called BW/4HANA with a roadmap to 2040+, triggering a timeline that will culminate in 2027 with the end of mainstream maintenace of the ABAP version BW7.5.

However, many customers for a variety of reasons chose not to move to BW4. Either they have too much logic built in objects not easily ported (for example APDs, BEx) or they were enticed by promises made by best of breed data vendors in cloud native data lake architectures to migrate their BW workload. Often these project spiral in costs and effort without delivering sufficient value.

The Solution

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), released in February 2025 is SAP’s fully managed SaaS solution built for modern workloads and to natively handle data-as-a-service from across the SAP Business Suite applications (S/4HANA Public and Private, SuccessFactors, Concur etc). This is achieved at the click of a button whereby ‘Data Products’ are loaded and kept in sync with backend transactional systems, removing the leg work for customers looking to get rapid value from their data. Business Content and Intelligent applications are installed via the BDC Cockpit, surfacing KPIs for decision making across a host of business areas, with a vast amount more to come in 2026. With BDC, SAP Databricks, SAP Snowflake and other best of breed solutions work in tandem with SAP Datasphere on the semantically rich data products providing customers a unified data architecture to deliver next generation A.I use cases.

What adds to the excitement of BW customers setting their new year goals? BDC is the go-to solution for SAP Business Warehouse. BDC provides a strong roadmap for customers to modernise their BW7.5 environment at their own pace, retaining their investments, their skills, keeping their data available and opening their rich sets of historic business data for A.I. This is achieved through the ‘Lift, Shift & Innovate’ approach that allows for large complex BWs to move to a managed cloud service (Private Cloud Edition) of BW within the formation of SAP Business Data Cloud.

The Lift phase moves existing SAP BW systems as-is to SAP-managed cloud infrastructure, extending maintenance timelines (2030 instead of 2027) and reducing operational overhead. The Shift phase exposes SAP BW data as Data Products using the Data Product Generator tool, enabling integration with SAP Datasphere and external data sources while preserving existing use cases. The Innovate phase explores SAP-managed Data Products and Intelligent Applications to replace legacy SAP BW scenarios with modern analytics, AI capabilities, and access to an open partner ecosystem. This phased approach mitigates risk by maintaining existing investments while providing a clear path to cloud-native analytics and reduced Total Cost of Ownership.

There are some prerequisites for your BW system to fulfil prior to embarking on the journey with BDC, the minimum version supporting the data product generator is SPS 24 and it needs to be running on HANA.

Many large global organisations reliant on BW for their strategic reporting, planning/business steering have already begun the transition to BDC to realise the benefits of modernisation to cloud native at their own pace.

‘Vibe Migration’

In Autumn/Winter 2025 the SAP EMEA and Global Solution Advisory teams worked cohesively on a pilot to demonstrate the BW to BDC move for a specific customer case.

This case assessed all aspects of a complex BW7.5 system after the Lift to PCE, demonstrating how the Shift and Innovate phases can look using the modern tools available in SAP BDC with a focus on SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud and Artificial Intelligence. Throughout the subsequent blog posts we will share the findings with specifics on how end of life (EoL) objects such as APDs, BEx and other scenarios can be covered either through automated migration to SAP BDC Datasphere, A.I assisted conversion or rebuild.

Please stay tuned for the next in this series of blog posts related to the Data Product Generator.

entry.title: 2026 SAP BTP GTM strategy and priority initiatives for Partners
entry.content:
If you’re planning your 2026 GTM motions, this is one session you’ll want on your calendar.

SAP Business Technology Platform is no longer just “the platform behind the scenes”.  It’s becoming the engine of a consumption-led, AI-first go-to-market strategy — and Partners play a central role in making that real for customers.

In this executive-level webinar, SAP’s BTP Customer Success and Ecosystem leaders will walk through how SAP Build, SAP Integration Suite, CIAM, and Core AI fit together as one coherent story.  Not in theory, but in actual GTM scenarios Partners can use to drive pipeline, bookings, and long-term consumption.

We’ll look at how SAP BTP shows up across RISE with SAP and GROW with SAP, why cACV matters more than ever, and how partners can align clean core principles with real customer outcomes.  Plus, we’ll share concrete partner-led GTM plays, and enablement programs designed to help you scale.

If you’re focused on sharpening your SAP BTP value proposition and building repeatable GTM motions for 2026, this session is for you.

👉 Register now  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/feb/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00012968.html

#SAP #SAPPartners #SAPBTP #PartnerGTM #SAPBuild #EnterpriseAI

entry.title: SAP Developer News, February 5th, 2026
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION

SAP Build Q4 2025 Release Highlights

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/tooling-sap-build-blog-posts/what-s-new-in-sap-build-q4-2025-release-highlights/bc-p/14316927

My Home in SAP BTP ABAP Environment

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/my-home-in-sap-btp-abap-environment/ba-p/14314918

 

Did You Know? #2 – Mocking Auth in CAP

Devtoberfest 2025 – Winners Announcement

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-blog-posts/devtoberfest-2025-winners-announcement/ba-p/14320779

New live stream series and blog post series on CAP topics

  • Modules, modularity & reuse in CDS models (blog post series) https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2026/01/01/modules-modularity-and-reuse-in-cds-models/
  • Hands-on SAP Dev CXL live stream series playlist https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQCEU8XcyqnA5wYEZGxMPm6B
  • A new Hands-on SAP Dev mini-series on the core expression language in CDS (blog post) https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2025/12/09/a-new-hands-on-sap-dev-mini-series-on-the-core-expression-language-in-cds/
  • Constraints, expressions and axioms in action (blog post) https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2026/01/27/constraints-expressions-and-axioms-in-action/

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 SAP Build Q4 2025 Release Highlights

1:36 My Home in SAP BTP ABAP Environment

3:04 DYK #2 Mocking Auth in CAP

4:10 Devtoberfest 2025 – Winners Announcement

4:44 New live stream series and blog post series on CAP topics

Transcription

00;00;00;29 - 00;00;05;28
Announcer Guy
This is the SAP Developer News for February 5th, 2026.

00;00;06;00 - 00;00;09;25
Shrini Neelamegam
Q4 of 2025 was a huge season for SAP Build,

00;00;09;27 - 00;00;40;11
Shrini Neelamegam
and we have got some powerful new features to talk about. First up, the agent builder in Joule Studio is officially GA. With MCP server integration and the ability to link multiple agents together. You can now build production grade AI agents that actually moves the needle for your business. Plus, Joule is now baked into every edition of Work Zone, bringing context aware answers and proactive insights right to your team's fingertips.

00;00;40;13 - 00;01;07;17
Shrini Neelamegam
For the developers out there, you can now spin up Freestyle UI5 apps in SAP Build Code with a simple slash command. And if you are looking to automate, you have added new ways to trigger processes and build process automation, making it easier to bridge the gap between SAP and non SAP systems. On the design front end SAP mobile start just got a makeover with Apple's liquid glass look.

00;01;07;20 - 00;01;35;14
Shrini Neelamegam
And for the admins, Workzone sites can now handle thousands of documents and translations. We are just scratching the surface here. There is plenty more on decision tables, decision rules, HTML templates for notifications, and new CAPannotations. Head over to Frederick's latest blog post for full Q4 2025 release announcement.

00;01;35;17 - 00;02;07;13
Sheena M K
SAP has rolled out My Home, the new personalized, light entry page for the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. By default, from at least 2602, and it will be the one and only entry page from release 2608. My home is designed to boost productivity with a modular, customizable layout that includes key sections such as news pages, add and insights, and cards, all aimed at giving users quick access to the tools and information they interact with the most.

00;02;07;15 - 00;02;40;00
Sheena M K
While the new section keeps you updated with the latest company or system updates, beta section offers quick access to your most used or favorite pages. The apps section improves discoverability with favorite, recent and most used apps. Plus, AI powered recommendations based on role assignments via SAP Business AI. The inside 30s provide immediate visibility into KPIs and analytics and data, offering quick access to apps for further details.

00;02;40;03 - 00;03;06;08
Sheena M K
Following the “Make It Mine” approach, users can hide, reorder and restructure sections. The skew then working side across all devices. So start exploring my home for a clean, organized and smarter navigation for the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. And for details, check out the blog post from Laura in the description below.

00;03;06;10 - 00;03;33;19
DJ Adams
Did you know that CAP has a mocked authentication strategy you can use in local development? So you don't have to put off this important part of your domain design? Here's a simple service annotated with some auth requirements. Authentication is required generally, and there are some role based restrictions on one of the entities. With CDS watch we get this strategy out of the box, as we can see, along with some users and roles too.

00;03;33;21 - 00;04;01;05
DJ Adams
Here's Alice with the admin role. For example. Let's explore. How about trying to read the Book Stocks Entity? 401! We need to authenticate, of course. Let's authenticate as Alice. Now we have read access. What about write? 403. Nope. Alice doesn't have the requisite role. Let's fix that with some extra config in dot cdsrc dot Json, for example, which assigns her the back office role that's required.

00;04;01;08 - 00;04;09;21
DJ Adams
Okay, let's try again. Success output request as Alice is now accepted. Nice.

00;04;09;23 - 00;04;31;17
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
Hey, SAP Community, it's finally time! We have the full list of the ten Devtoberfest 2025 winners ready and online. You can find a link to the blog post down in the description of this video, so you can head over to the SAP community and see if you are one of the approved winners of last year's Devtoberfest.

00;04;31;20 - 00;04;42;27
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
And if you're one of the winners, you can look forward to a great fully paid trip to SAP TechEd this year. I can't wait to see you there!

00;04;43;00 - 00;05;21;13
DJ Adams
The developer advocates mission is to help developers. That's you be successful. To that end, we create lots of content in various forms CodeJam, tutorials, videos, talks, and lots more. We just wanted to highlight a couple of recent series in the CAP area. First, there's a blog post series on the topic of CDS modeling. The series is called Modules Modularity and Reuse in CDS models, and it explores how we can use modules for reuse in and across our projects and beyond.

00;05;21;15 - 00;05;53;15
DJ Adams
It illustrates this by first creating and publishing a simple passive reuse module before moving on to understand, explore and dig into the active Capire common reuse module. We're also running a series right now on our hands on SAP Devs live stream show. It's on CAP’s Expression Language, C XL, and we're very happy to have Patrice Bender from the Cap compiler team take us through the beautiful detail in this show and tell style series.

00;05;53;17 - 00;06;21;10
DJ Adams
As always, driven by curiosity and the desire to understand. By the way, there's also a related blog post just published that explains some of the amazing detail that Patrice revealed in showing how constraints are realized by pushing down to the database. This week sees the third livestream episode in this series. You can join live at the usual hands on SAP Dev time.

00;06;21;10 - 00;06;29;09
DJ Adams
It's Friday mornings in Europe, and of course you can always watch the replays at your leisure links in the description.

entry.title: Future proofing enterprise agility with SAP Integration Suite and clean core
entry.content:
Enterprise agility sounds great on paper.  In reality?  It often feels like a constant balancing act between innovation and stability.

The thing is, many organizations are still trying to move fast while carrying years of technical debt.  That’s where SAP’s clean core philosophy — powered by SAP Integration Suite — really starts to make a difference.

In this upcoming partner session, we’ll walk through how clean core integrations and modern integration patterns help enterprises stay flexible without compromising their core.  You’ll hear practical guidance, real-world examples, and lessons learned from customers who are actively modernizing their landscapes today.

If you work with customers navigating complex integrations, hybrid environments, or long-term transformation roadmaps, this session is designed with you in mind.  Honestly, it’s about building agility that lasts — not quick fixes that create tomorrow’s problems.

Join us to explore how a future-proof integration strategy can unlock real business value, while keeping the core clean.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/feb/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00012943.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPIntegrationSuite #CleanCore #EnterpriseIntegration

entry.title: New course: Positioning SAP Order Management Services
entry.content:
We’re excited to introduce a brand-new learning opportunity for sales and pre-sales professionals:

Positioning SAP Order Management Services 

Why SAP Order Management Services?

In today’s digital-first economy, businesses face increasing challenges in managing their order processes. From handling multiple sales channels and meeting rising customer expectations to integrating with legacy systems and reducing errors, order management has never been more complex. SAP Order Management Services (OMS) is engineered to solve exactly these challenges.

What Will You Learn?

This focused course goes beyond product features. It guides you through:

  • The challenges faced by modern businesses in order management: Understand the pain points your customers are experiencing, such as supply chain visibility gaps, inefficiencies in order handling, and the need for real-time inventory management.
  • The end-to-end capabilities of SAP OMS: Discover how SAP OMS covers the full order process lifecycle, from order capture and validation to fulfillment, tracking, and returns, seamlessly integrated across all channels and touchpoints.
  • Business value drivers and tangible benefits: Learn how to articulate the business case, showing customers how SAP OMS improves operational efficiency, elevates the customer experience, reduces costs, boosts revenue, and supports scalability and innovation.
  • Inspiring customer success stories: See how real-world organizations have transformed their order management processes and driven measurable business outcomes with SAP OMS, stories you can share to build trust and credibility with your clients.

Who Should Enroll?

  • Sales consultants working with SAP customers or prospects
  • Pre-sales consultants looking to stand out with specialized knowledge
  • Anyone eager to understand how SAP OMS drives smarter business operations

Learn Today!

Ready to master SAP OMS positioning? Start learning now and take the first step toward equipping yourself with the knowledge and strategies you need to confidently position SAP Order Management Services.

 

We hope you enjoy this course and encourage you to share your valuable feedback with us. Thank you!

entry.title: Joule Is Not a Chatbot: How AI Agents on SAP BTP Help North American Utilities Reduce Risk
entry.content:
Introduction

 In North American utilities, regulatory expectations shape almost every operational decision. Over the years working with U.S. and Canadian utilities, I’ve seen how state and provincial rules influence even the smallest processes — from how quickly meter reads must be validated to how consistently billing exceptions must be resolved.

Regulators typically define:

  • Required timelines for completing meter reads

  • SLA windows for resolving billing exceptions

  • Limits on consecutive estimated bills

  • Documentation standards for every action taken

When these expectations aren’t met, the consequences are real: customer credits, revenue adjustments, additional reporting, and increased regulatory scrutiny. Even with strong CIS and MDM systems, I’ve seen exception queues grow faster than teams can manage. SLA timers often run in the background without real-time visibility, and escalations still depend heavily on someone noticing an issue manually.

This gap between regulatory expectations and operational reality is exactly where AI agents can make a meaningful difference.

Main Content

1. Regulatory Context for North American Utilities

In North American utilities, regulatory expectations shape almost every operational decision. Over the years working with U.S. and Canadian utilities, I’ve seen how state and provincial rules influence even the smallest processes — from how quickly meter reads must be validated to how consistently billing exceptions must be resolved.

Regulators typically define required timelines, SLA windows, limits on estimated bills, and documentation standards. When these expectations aren’t met, utilities face customer credits, revenue adjustments, additional reporting, and increased scrutiny. Exception queues grow quickly, SLA timers run without visibility, and escalations depend on manual intervention.

This is where AI agents can make a meaningful difference.

2. Why Joule Is More Than a Chatbot

During my learning journey with Joule, one misconception stood out immediately: many people assume Joule is “just another chatbot.” But when you look at how it works on SAP BTP, it becomes clear that Joule is fundamentally different.

A chatbot answers questions. A Joule Agent takes action.

From a practitioner’s perspective, what makes Joule valuable is its ability to:

  • Understand business rules and regulatory requirements

  • Monitor operational data continuously

  • Detect exceptions before they breach SLAs

  • Trigger automated workflows

  • Maintain complete audit logs for compliance

In regulated industries like utilities, this shift from conversational assistance to policy-driven automation is significant. It’s not about chatting — it’s about operational discipline.

 

3. Why AI Agents Matter for Exception Management

Exception management has always been one of the most resource-intensive areas in utilities. Across multiple programs, I’ve seen the same patterns repeat:

  • No-read or zero-consumption meters

  • High/low usage variances

  • Billing holds

  • Field-service-dependent issues

  • High-bill complaints

Each of these carries its own SLA clock. When thousands of exceptions accumulate, manual monitoring simply can’t keep up — even with experienced teams.

AI agents help by addressing the exact pain points I’ve seen utilities struggle with:

3.1 Continuous Monitoring

Agents watch exceptions as they occur, not hours or days later.

3.2 Risk-Based Prioritization

They highlight issues that could cause regulatory or revenue impact — something analysts often have to do manually.

3.3 Autonomous Escalation

They trigger workflows or notify supervisors without waiting for human review, reducing the dependency on tribal knowledge.

 

4. How Joule Agents Work on SAP BTP

A Joule Agent is not a standalone tool — it’s an orchestrator that brings multiple BTP services together.

4.1 Data and Event Integration

It listens to events from systems such as:

  • SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • SAP MDM

  • SAP Event Mesh

  • SAP HANA Cloud

In real operations, these events include new exceptions, updated SLA timers, meter reads, or billing document changes.

4.2 Policy and Rule Interpretation

Regulatory rules are translated into thresholds, categories, and escalation paths — something utilities typically manage through spreadsheets or manual SOPs.

4.3 Workflow Execution

The agent can start SAP Build Process Automation workflows, reassign cases, or notify supervisors.

4.4 Audit Logging

Every action is logged for compliance — a major benefit for regulatory reporting.

 

5. Practical Example: SLA Monitoring for Meter-Reading Exceptions

Here’s a scenario I’ve seen repeatedly: A utility must resolve meter-reading exceptions within 48 hours. These include no-reads, communication failures, and high/low variances.

Without AI Agents

  • Exceptions pile up

  • Analysts manually review queues

  • Supervisors rely on periodic reports

  • SLA timers aren’t monitored continuously

  • Escalations depend on human judgment

This often leads to missed deadlines and inconsistent documentation — especially during peak seasons.

 

Figure 1: SLA monitoring lifecycle for meter-reading exceptions using Joule Agents.

 

With a Joule Agent

Step 1: Monitors exceptions and SLA timers in real time Step 2: Flags high-risk or time-sensitive cases Step 3: Automatically triggers workflows, reassigns cases, and notifies supervisors Step 4: Captures all actions for compliance

Outcome:

  • Fewer SLA breaches

  • Less revenue leakage

  • Stronger compliance

  • Better use of staff time

From a practitioner’s perspective, this is exactly the kind of automation utilities have been trying to achieve for years.

 

6. Reference Architecture

A typical setup includes:

  • SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • SAP MDM

  • SAP Event Mesh

  • SAP HANA Cloud

  • Joule Agent

  • SAP Build Process Automation

  • SAP Audit Log Service

This architecture can be rolled out gradually and adapted to existing landscapes — something important for utilities with complex legacy environments.

 
Figure 2: Reference architecture for Joule Agent orchestration across SAP BTP services.

 

7. Governance and Compliance

Regulators expect utilities to demonstrate:

  • Proactive monitoring

  • Consistent rule application

  • Clear escalation paths

  • Strong audit evidence

Joule Agents help by applying rules consistently, reducing manual errors, and maintaining detailed logs — all of which strengthen compliance posture.

 

8. Organizational Impact

Utilities that adopt AI agents typically see improvements in:

  • Exception resolution speed

  • SLA compliance

  • Revenue protection

  • Operational efficiency

  • Employee workload balance

Supervisors gain real-time visibility, analysts focus on solving issues instead of chasing timers, and compliance teams get better documentation.

 

Conclusion

Joule is not a chatbot. It’s an AI agent framework designed to help utilities automate compliance, reduce revenue leakage, and manage exceptions proactively. Based on what I’ve seen across multiple utility programs, the shift from reactive exception handling to automated, risk-aware operations is becoming essential — and Joule Agents on SAP BTP provide a practical path to get there.

entry.title: Kick Off 2026 With Impact: Collaborate on 56 Fresh SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Projects
entry.content:
We’re excited to announce that Cycle 1 of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative for 2026 is now live!
This cycle features 56 new projects, offering SAP customers and partners the opportunity to gain early insights into upcoming product developments and collaborate directly with SAP teams to shape the next generation of SAP solutions.

🗓Registration is open until March 13, 2026.

In a rapidly evolving business environment, early involvement can make a meaningful difference. Through the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative, you can work directly with SAP product managers and developers, sharing your feedback on concepts, prototypes, and plans while they are still in the early stages.

To help you explore all available opportunities, we’ve prepared a complete overview of the projects.

Others

Let’s shape the future together!

Innovation happens through collaboration. By participating in the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative projects, you help SAP create better, more relevant solutions for businesses like yours. This is your chance to influence what comes next.

Don’t miss out – sign up today at influence.sap.com and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 1 in 2026!

Warm regards, 
Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team 

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.
